data.title: SDIV: The ETF That Pays You To Lose Money
data.description:
For income focused investors, portfolio growth is not optional. Emphasizing on yields that are tax friendly and higher payouts as a focus is okay. But where do you fund those payouts from? That is a key problem many high yield or income strategies turn a blind eye to. If there is no growth in the portfolio, the dividends being paid out are nothing but a return of the invested capital.

The Global X SuperDividend ETF (NYSEARCA:SDIV) is one such ETF that promise and delivers the high dividends, but the underlying capital goes through systematic erosion over time. So, while a 10% yield on $100 investment returns $10 initially, within 5 years, the returns taper to $5 even at the same yield because the portfolio value has shrunk to $50. The issue with SDIV is not because it focuses on yield. The issue is the lack of balance between yield and quality picks that make payouts more sustainable in the longer run. I do not see value in the SDIV proposition as a long-term income investor. Tactical uses can be evaluated.

Methodology and Portfolio

SDIV replicates the Solactive Global SuperDividend Index. The methodology of the index is simple. It is a simple equal weighted collection of the top dividend yielding stocks globally. This includes the US and emerging markets. It also allows REITs and MLPs.

The offshoot of the methodology is the obvious high yield which is the primary objective. The TTM dividend yield is now at 9.9%. Monthly dividend payout is also an attractive structure for dividend focused investors. The dividend focus does not bias the portfolio geographically, although sectoral tilts may be expected. Overall, diversification should not be a concern and concentration risks are also mitigated by an equal weighted approach.

There are some constraints on the selection universe that mitigates risks to an extent. There are minimum liquidity and market cap qualification criteria. There is a minimum yield criteria of 6% for a stock to qualify because of the ETFs objective, which is very high in itself. There is also a high cut off at 20% to prevent riskier bets from seeping in. The selection process also looks at any announcements or outlook changes to the dividend to proactively screen inclusions.

The screens are not sufficient to ensure quality holdings for the portfolio. A 20% yield for cut-off does not serve its purpose in trimming riskier positions. A quarterly rebalance may still be acceptable if the underlying shares are financially strong. Most importantly, there is no filter at all to ensure some quality check is in place for the portfolio. Even a market cap selection criteria often works as a screen - here its role is limited to qualification only. And there are no other fundamental filters as well.

The resultant portfolio is as expected of the methodology. The concentration in the US is healthy at less than 25%. Among other markets, there is no bias between developed or emerging economies. I see a healthy presence of Brazil, China and Britain - showing a decent allocation in developed as well as emerging economies.

The sectoral allocation does have a heavy concentration in energy followed by mortgage REITs and financials. Even REITs are have decent weighting. The concentration on REITs, mortgage REITs and real estate makes the portfolio heavily dependent on the economic cycles. Energy allocations make the portfolio more volatile and, if not layered with quality factors often speculative. A true dividend portfolio, even if simply chasing yields should have a heavier concentration in financials where the risk is much reduced overall and long-term growth is a possibility.

I will refrain from posting the top 10 holdings and commenting on that because this is an equal weighted portfolio with changes in allocations due to share price changes between rebalances. You can take a look at all the holdings here. I did and saw some currency positions to hedge against currency risks. Among stocks, my subjective judgement saw only 10-15% exposure in names I would call quality with a yield focus - names like say Parex, Guess?, OPAP, Phoenix and Vale, among a few others. SDIV's focus is naturally tilted towards small and mid caps compared to the large cap concentration in most other yield ETFs, even the international ones. The holdings naturally show significant exposure to high leveraged financials, high payout cyclicals and even distressed or special situation names. SDIV is almost a maverick among yield focused plays, even including the international ones like VYMI.

Performance Drags

Empirical performance data manifests all the concerns the methodology points to. The high-risk high-yield approach could have worked out better in at least a few eras, but even a few years of consistent total return trends are missing. In fact, the total return numbers make the Sell case for SDIV even worse. I have compared SDIV's total returns against a random basket of yield ETFs for perspective. SPYD is completely US focused and quite conservative given that it starts with a large cap universe already filtered by the broader S&P 500 methodology. HDV (again US focused) is the most conservative in that it has a high-quality screen for its yield focus. VYMI is international, but has a larger portfolio and also screens for dividend sustainability. VDIV is also a global exposure fund with an eye on dividend history and stability. The methodology (reflected in total returns) of all the yield focused ETFs has its own strengths and pitfalls, but does have a healthy total return in the long term. SDIV is flat with barely a few years of total return growth that has never sustained.

The yield focus is clearly hurting the portfolio for SDIV. It goes to show how a balanced yield is often the best way to sustain dividends in the long term. The cost for higher yield pays off negatively and by a margin beyond a point. SDIV's NAV has almost consistently eroded with brief periods of stability. Yields have consistently remained high, and in fact move in negative correlation to the capital investment, as expected.

The expense ratio of 0.58% is not concerning, but its higher expense ratio to VYMI at 0.17%, HDV at 0.08%, SPYD at 0.08% or even WDIV at 0.4%, is far higher for no apparent advantageous positioning.

No Case for SDIV

A volatile risky growth stocks is still okay to ride, if I have researched it fundamentally and know my entry exit triggers. A volatile risky portfolio that is passive and not in my control is difficult to Buy. Historical performance adds to the overall Sell thesis. For passive ETFs, a double digit yield is often unsustainable. For DIY approaches, that can still be attempted, balancing quality alongside yield considerations and staying agile on execution. SDIV doesn't just underperform other yield alternatives (lower yields but sustainable), it returns close to nothing over the long term.

Even for tactical use, I cannot recommend SDIV because it doesn't follow a theme that can be predicted to be rewarding in some market conditions. At most, one can count on risk-on rallies, lower interest growth regimes where broader markets remain upbeat. That is not the current regime surely. To SDIV's defense, the total returns have been relatively better of in the past 2-3 years. But don't rely on chances. SDIV's methodology looks very speculative and have historically seen eventual erosion even after brief periods of relative, just about acceptable resilience.

data.title: Royal Gold: A Gold Royalty Giant Going Through Massive Acquisitions, But Lagging Peers
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Royal Gold (NASDAQ:RGLD) is one of the largest royalty and streaming companies, with a focus predominantly on gold, silver and especially copper, which has been going through significant acquisitions in recent months. Right now, the company seems poised for significant growth ahead, while the stock has been lagging behind most of its peers, being up ~30% YTD compared to ~50% to 70% recorded by their main peers, which is in line with gold’s ~28.4% return. However, as streamers offer a middle ground when it comes to leverage on gold (miners being the higher tier, with more risk and reward), and given the company’s recent acquisitions that I’ll mention below, this seems like an unjustified response from the market.

On top of this, the company is quite attractive from a geographical diversification point of view, with ~28% of NAV in Canada and ~13% in the US prior to a deal that will add exposure to Zambia as well that I’ll mention later, being one of the best portfolio concentrations from a jurisdiction risk point of view compared to peers. Back in 2024, around 60% of their revenue came from Canada, USA and Australia, which are considered to be among the highest-quality jurisdictions for mining.

As they say, they are large enough to grow and compete in high-stakes deals, but also small enough to still have room to grow significantly as their investments pay off. Given the gold equivalent ounces guidance of 330,000 to 375,000 expected in 2025 in the image above (which they maintained following the latest earnings report; which will be higher following a recent deal that should add 35,000 to 40,000 ounces per year), we’re looking at a ~$11.34 billion market cap, while competitors who expect to produce less than twice this much come at almost 3 to 4 times higher market caps.

As you can expect, their Q2 report came with records for pretty much everything, from the revenue of $209.6 million, operating cash flow of $152.8 million and the earnings of $132.3 million. Their free cash flow hasn’t really been amazing for this market cap, but we can see an overall solid one over the past decade, with significant drops when the company announces massive CAPEX used for deals - and it’s overall in line with peers.

In this case, the net income might offer the clearer picture, showing how the company has more than quadrupled from the levels seen back in 2017 and 2019, on the back of the jump in gold/silver prices and growth in their attributable production, which peaked a couple of years ago but is now poised for a rebound, as mentioned below.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see an excellent position (similar to most streamers), with the current assets covering the total liabilities and zero debt. However, we’re looking at ~$3.32 billion in equity on a $11.34 billion market cap, and although the balance sheet is backward-looking when it comes to the value of the assets (the NAV shows their current value), we’re still talking about a high price to pay for their net assets. This is normal when gold is trading near its highest levels in history, but could represent a risk if prices fall. However, that’s for a normal company, not a gold streamer. The business model is very solid and helps the company return a profit almost no matter the environment, and the lack of debt means there’s no need for them to sell in desperation if the environment deteriorates - unlike some miners.

However, as I’ll mention below, the company will access an $875 million credit facility, which they will likely repay in the near future as they did before with other debt cycles. As you can see in the image above, this is far from their first draw, and I like that they focus on repaying rather than letting it mount up to unsustainable levels, which adds to the safety of this business model.

Dividends & Buybacks

Similar to peers, their dividend yield is far from good, especially after the stock went up. As they are generally paying the equivalent of a ~20% payout ratio, this will very likely increase as a result of the significant jumps we’ve seen this year. This would mark their 25th annual dividend increase, but since they aren’t an S&P 500 component, they are technically not going to be a Dividend Aristocrat, although they’re part of the broader S&P High Yield Dividend Aristocrats Index (only requires 20 years of dividend increases)

Despite a ~15% dividend CAGR from 2000 to 2025, the company still pays a yield below inflation, but again, that’s because streamers are a middle ground between gold itself and miners. They come with some leverage on gold plus a dividend (in this case, below inflation), while gold doesn’t pay anything. Streamers, on the other hand, are businesses that can compound thanks to their investments, and buying this back a decade ago would result in ~3% yields on cost, as well as a 3x return, although we’re talking about the lowest and highest points for the commodity, so it’s not necessarily a fair comparison for the long-term.

Risks & Opportunities

In my Sandstorm Gold (SAND) analysis published recently, I mentioned how the company’s plans to double their attributable gold production by 2030 combined with a low valuation compared to peers despite doubling over the past year make it a Buy.

Less than a week later, Royal Gold made the, in my opinion, wise decision of buying them together with Horizon Copper (OTCQX:HNCUF) - which was ~34% owned by Sandstorm - in a deal valued at more than $3.5 billion in total. This adds very significant growth through gold and copper exposure from a diversified set of countries to Royal Gold, and I think this might be one of the best mergers/acquisitions we’ve seen this year (so far) and it’s quite tough to beat.

Upon closing, the existing RGLD and SAND shareholders will own ~77% and ~23%, of RGLD’s issued and outstanding shares on a fully diluted basis, which is seen as an issue in the market, hence the ~5.6% decline on the announcement. Still, looking at it from a long-term perspective, we’re talking about a company with a massive growth pipeline which was bought before it could really show what it can truly do.

Yes, this comes with dilution and the market wasn’t very happy about it, but we have to remember that when you buy cashflow-producing assets, the per share results can even be accretive, depending on how good the deal was. In Sandstorm’s case, we’re talking about a royalty company that’s expecting to double its attributable production by 2030, which is well above what Wheaton (WPM) and Franco-Nevada (FNV) expect - both of which I recently analyzed.

As RGLD says, this deal is even accretive to the Net Asset Value for the company, estimating a 44% increase in NAV from this deal, as Sandstorm was trading at a low P/NAV. So, this dilution results in a massive increase in NAV, as well as an immediate ~26% increase in 2025’s production (without even including Horizon), plus Sandstorm’s pipeline to double it by 2030, which is an amazing deal and quite a steal for Sandstorm shareholders, although they’ll get shares in the combined company and can still enjoy its future.

Shortly after announcing this deal, they also announced acquiring a gold stream on First Quantum’s flagship Kansanshi copper-gold mine in Zambia for $1 billion, paying from 20% to 35% of the spot gold price for each ounce delivered, depending on certain conditions described in the press release. This could be somewhat risky, as $1 billion is very significant for a now $11.34 billion market cap due to the country they gain exposure to and the fact that $875 million of that will be funded through a credit facility and $125 million through cash, but the overall business remains one of the most Americas and gold focused streamers. And as a note, these two were not the only deals RGLD got into this year.

With streamers, I believe their ability to issue stock and debt is one of their strongest points. As the companies don’t make a lot of cash for it to lead to significant investments, these external sources of capital can offer the key to their growth, as we can see with deals such as the ones above. Unlike miners, who sometimes trade at some of their lowest points depending on the commodity cycle, streamers often come at very high ratios thanks to their higher stability.

As for the long-term, I remain bullish both on gold and especially copper, and I believe their diversification into copper is actually a very good move. When it comes to gold, I believe this commodity’s potential is nearly unlimited, as it is not tied to “real life” sources of demand that much compared to any other metal, including precious ones. We can see that almost half of the demand for gold comes from investment and central banks, while a majority comes from jewelry, none of these being the most rational or elastic sources of demand. In short, higher gold prices wouldn’t make investors or central banks want to buy less or diminish jewelry demand significantly, and they could even lead to an increase in demand especially if we get into a fear of missing out or hype scenario. If you think about it, with all the tariffs, rate cuts devaluing the dollar, uncertainty, wars, etc., the fact that precious metals went up so much this year shouldn’t be a surprise.

To that, add the fact that the median AISC for gold has surpassed $1,500 in Q1 2025, which is more than 50% higher than Q4 2020. This means that more than half of the gold produced is now coming at an AISC above $1,500 per ounce, which is very significant for the future prices of the metal, as a drop to that level - or even $2,000 or more - could make a huge chunk of the production unsustainable, meaning mines closing, bankruptcies, production cuts, etc. Back in 2016-2018, it’s no surprise that we were seeing gold at a bit over $1,000 or $1,200 per ounce, as that level was enough for a wide majority of mines to produce gold at a profit. Now however, that level would imply that about 80-85% of the production wouldn’t be profitable, which is nowhere near a sustainable level, theoretically causing a massive drop in supply.

As I mentioned in the recent Teck Resources (TECK) stock analysis, copper on the other hand is much more tied to the economy - especially China, which accounts for over 50% of total copper demand! Today, a majority of copper demand comes from Electrical and Electronics (~35-40%), Construction and Infrastructure (25-30%), Transportation (~15%) and other uses. This is a main reason why it’s also called “doctor copper” because it’s usually a very good representation of the economy, as demand for copper depends on the state of the global economy. But, the main thing about copper is that the demand is expected to grow significantly thanks to clean energy transition and storage, EVs and charging, data centers, etc., while production is expected to struggle to keep up.

However, since China represents a significant source of demand and an even higher refiner, we can see this leading to potential issues caused by tariffs - if they continue - so it’s kind of a double-edged sword, and we’ve seen copper prices dropping by a historic ~22% in a single day back at the end of July when Trump “eased” copper tariffs. This relationship with the economy means that on the one hand, good times can be very beneficial for the demand and price, while the bad ones can lead to very significant crashes, as we’ve seen many times before.

As a final point here, I believe it’s important to keep in mind that these assets are long-term investments. What this stock does is basically offer exposure to some of the best mines on the planet, but that too is something that shows results over time. Especially in Sandstorm’s case, this deal will likely be very accretive especially over the next half a decade, when their projects and deals begin to show the effects of significant ramp-ups, but time and patience remain very important with such an investment. However, in this environment, RGLD might be quite an opportunity now after buying Sandstorm at an attractive valuation, and their exposure to copper may be something that will help a lot over the next decade.

Peer Comparison

Compared to their main peers, Franco-Nevada (FNV), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), OR Royalties (OR) and Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM), we actually see the best valuation metrics in pretty much every regard. I also included Sandstorm Gold (SAND), to show how they will consolidate their level at some of the lowest ratios even after this transaction, plus the very good growth potential the deal comes with.

Valuation

From the midpoint of ~352,500 GEO expected this year, if we add the difference from First Quantum to get to the ~35,000 - 40,000 GEO they expect, as well as ~75,000 increase from Sandstorm, we’re looking at over 450,000 GEO by 2030 just from these deals alone, without accounting for any growth in their own projects before these acquisitions. Rounding it to 500,000 to assume a less than 3% CAGR for their other projects, we’re looking at an increase of ~42.85% in their estimated GEO compared to 2025, and about 66.5% higher compared to 2024, which is in line with their peers.

As a result of ~40% higher gold prices this year compared to 2024’s averages and the potential to see solid prices throughout the long-term, we could easily be looking at a company that’s making even more than $730 million in free cash flows by 2030, although it ultimately depends on the price of gold.

I will assume a starting FCF of $317.7 million in 2024, a CAGR that brings the FCF to $730 million in 2029 (~18.06%), a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years as their pipeline would naturally fall and they’d have to find new projects, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a WACC of 7% (assumes: 7.15% cost of equity, 4.3% cost of debt, ~17.5% effective tax rate; takes into account the $825 million credit facility). This way, we get to an enterprise value of approximately $12.55 billion, and when subtracting the $727 million in net debt (following the financing of the First Quantum deal through cash and debt), this returns an equity value of roughly $11.82 billion. With 85 million shares outstanding (following the Sandstorm Gold deal), this gives an intrinsic value of about $139.06 per share, which is ~17.5% below the current price, so the price reflects all these potential achievements quite well.

Conclusions

Overall, I rate Royal Gold a Hold now, as the stock is likely pricing in their significant deals and the price of gold, with most of the potential upside above this estimate coming from better gold prices and deals in the future. The company remains valued quite well compared to their peers however, and a recovery in that to the industry averages could also offer some potential, although it doesn’t seem that significant to justify a buy with a good margin of safety. If anything, these recent acquisitions got them in line with their peers in terms of growth potential. Still, now with Sandstorm being taken out of the equation, they remain one of the better royalty and streaming companies in the market, so definitely something to keep an eye on for potential dips.

Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit jhinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington’s new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop.

Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lower- quality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation detracted from results, as our diversified approach was out of step with the aggressive rally. The real assets portfolio was the primary detractor due to negative returns for energy stocks and U.S. real estate investment trusts. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms — also weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. On the positive side, the fund benefited from having an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. Allocation in the fixed-income portfolio was a small contributor, primarily as a result of an overweight in emerging-market bonds.

Underlying manager performance detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the equity portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors’ rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don’t attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.

data.title: Cigna: Market Pessimism Creates Value Opportunity
data.description:
Investment thesis

After the report for the second quarter of 2025, the market reacted with a sharp drop, but I believe that the reaction was excessive. Cigna (NYSE:CI) is currently trading at a serious discount, and even to its average over the past five years, the company is about 15% cheaper, which suggests that the market is putting extra pessimism into the price. I updated the DCF model (my last article), took into account the latest data, and slightly lowered my margin and profit growth forecasts, but the key potential has not gone away. My new target price is $315 per share, which gives about 8.5% upside. With the Buy rating, the idea is simple — the company has steady cash flows, an active capital return program to shareholders, and an assessment that, in my opinion, does not reflect its long-term capabilities. After this drawdown, the risk/return ratio became in favor of the buyer, and at such levels, stocks look like an interesting entry point.

FY Guidance Intact Despite Mixed Q2

In the second quarter of 2025, the picture turned out to be ambiguous. Revenue turned out to be higher than expected — plus 11% compared to last year, to $67.2 billion, and the main contribution was made by Evernorth, where the volume of services in the pharmaceutical network and the sale of specialty drugs increased. But GAAP net income was almost unchanged: $1.53 billion, or $5.71 per share, compared to $1.55 billion and $5.45 per share a year ago. It turned out that revenue accelerated, but profit stood still — and this, along with the fact that the report became more difficult to read due to the sale of the Medicare business, provoked a negative reaction from investors.

In the second quarter of 2025, Evernorth Health Services again showed a good pace — revenue increased by 17% compared to last year, and pre-tax profit increased by 5%. Specialty & Care Services also added — plus 13% to revenue and plus 7% to profit due to an increase in the volume of rare drugs and biosimilars, although the return on investment decreased slightly. Overall, Evernorth is steadily growing in volume, but the margin has dropped to 2.9% from 3.3% a year ago, and this is due to the fact that there is more low-margin PBM in the structure and the business continues to scale.

Cigna Healthcare, as expected, showed a drop due to the sale of Medicare - revenue decreased by 18%. If we remove this factor, the segment still grew by 7% due to higher insurance premiums in order to cover the growth of medical expenses. Without one—time reserves, the MCR went roughly according to plan, and the abandonment of low-margin Medicare even slightly improved the margin - 10.2% versus 9.2% a year ago. But the insurance segment is still vulnerable: the market reacts sharply to any signals of rising costs, and even the predicted deterioration in the MCR has caused a cautious reaction.

In the first half of the year, the company has already earned $13.94 per share, which means that in the second half it needs to show $15.7 to fulfill the plan. In the third quarter, I expect revenue to continue to grow at high single-digit rates due to the expansion of Evernorth and the indexation of insurance premiums. The margin of the insurance business may recover slightly after the peak stop-loss payments in the second quarter, so profits are likely to show moderate year-on-year growth. Conservatively, I'm putting adjusted EPS in the range of $7.0–7.5, which should be enough to meet or even slightly exceed the annual target. There are risks — fluctuations in investment income and seasonality of expenses in the fourth quarter — but at the moment the goals for 2025 look realistic.

Undervalued Despite Forecast Moderation

After the report for the second quarter of 2025, I recalculated DCF Cigna. Now I expect that in 2025-2030, revenue will grow by an average of 5% per year, and the operating margin will gradually rise from 3.9% in 2024-2025 to 4.2% by 2027. Capex will remain low, at about 0.7% of revenue, but amortization of intangible assets will increase: this will slightly reduce the net profit, but it will support the free flow. More will have to be invested in working capital in 2025-2026 due to the growth of the pharmacy segment, but by 2027 the situation will return to normal, and some of the money will be released. In general, even with more restrained expectations, the company will still generate large amounts of available funds, which keeps a fair estimate afloat.

The WACC for the company has not changed significantly since my last article and, according to my calculations, is about 5.61% (the prerequisites are indicated in the table above).

Since the last valuation, Cigna's multiples have generally declined, which suggests that the market has begun to value it even cheaper. The Non-GAAP forward P/E fell from 10.59 to 9.68— minus 8.6%. EV/Sales (FWD) also sank slightly, from 0.41 to 0.39, and remains almost 89% below the sectoral level. This means that while maintaining the fundamental stability of the business, the market has begun to evaluate the company even more conservatively, which increases the upside potential.

The previous estimate was higher (the target level was around $350), but the revision of the model reduced FCF forecasts, especially for the coming years. It is worth emphasizing that the model remains quite conservative (for example, the long-term margin is below the management goals of 5% and the historical peak of 5.5%). My current estimate of a fair price of $315 provides for multipliers when calculating the terminal value at 10x P/E 2025 and 8x EV/EBITDA, which is still below the sector average, but reflects the risks. I keep the Buy rating.

Aggressive Buybacks Driving EPS Growth

At the beginning of 2025, Cigna continued to actively buy up its shares. In January and February, the company bought 3 million shares for $901 million, and at the end of February it still had a reserve under the mandate for $9.4 billion, which is about 10% of the total capitalization. Management explicitly said that a significant portion of the $3.7 billion raised from the sale of the Medicare business would be used to continue the aggressive buyback. According to calculations, this will allow 2-4% of shares to be removed from the market per year, which in itself will add about 1-2 percentage points to earnings per share growth in excess of operating results. Payments to shareholders are made not only through buybacks. In the first quarter, the dividend was raised from $1.40 to $1.51 per share. At the same time, the payout ratio is approximately 30% of adjusted earnings, so the company has enough margin to raise dividends further. Currently, the priority in the distribution of free flow is the purchase of shares. At the same time, the company keeps its debt burden under control and improves metrics by increasing EBITDA.

Risks

Cigna, like any large company in American healthcare, has enough risks that can hit its valuation and profits. The main thing is regulation: the law in this area changes frequently, and now Evernorth's PBM business is under the gun. There are talks about new pricing rules, transparency of discounts, and bans on certain practices, which could cut margins. The competition is also serious: UnitedHealth, Anthem, CVS/Aetna, Humana are under pressure in insurance, and OptumRx, CVS Caremark, and even Amazon are in PBM. Another threat is medical inflation. If treatment, medications, or the frequency of referrals rise above the prescribed levels, the MCR will go up, as it already did in the second quarter of 2025. Errors in risk assessment when awarding premiums can also be expensive, especially in the context of unexpected epidemics or the emergence of ultra-expensive treatments. All this does not remove the main fact: the risks are mostly external, and Cigna is trying to smooth them out — it diversifies the business, keeps high cash flows and reduces dependence on government programs. But investors should keep their finger on the pulse: monitor the dynamics of MCR, PBM initiatives, the mood of key customers and debt indicators.

Final Thoughts

In the second quarter of 2025, Cigna had some weaknesses that made the market nervous for a while, but overall the picture remains healthy. At the current price, the market seems to be looking at the company too gloomily, although a more realistic scenario is a moderately positive one. My DCF and a comparison with the sector give a fair price of $315 per share, that is, an upside of about 8-9%. Yes, it has become smaller than I expected before, but it still covers the risks, and I leave the Buy recommendation. Cigna is a profitable and diversified business with a strong cash flow that actively returns capital to shareholders through buybacks and dividends. If the margin of the insurance segment recovers and Evernorth continues to expand, the company's results will tighten and the multipliers will return to more normal levels.

data.title: Pfizer: This Is What Bulls Waited For
data.description:
My Thesis Update

My very first article on Pfizer Inc. (NYSE:PFE)(NEOE:PFE:CA) stock came out in mid-February 2024 with a "Buy" rating as I was arguing that PFE's depressed valuation and strong product pipeline made it "the ultimate buying opportunity for income investors in the healthcare sector." After months of relative (and absolute) underperformance of PFE, I decided to keep my bullish thesis on the stock in my most recent article, published in late May 2025, when I argued that Pfizer's active investing in its pipeline, especially oncology and vaccines, should eventually help it offset the LOEs' hit to the top and bottom lines. So far, it's early to say that my latest take was indeed correct, but PFE stock's reversal seems to be gaining ground:

After the report that came out in early August, we see that the sentiment has finally shifted, and the progress that the firm has shown in the pipeline and cost-cutting is not visible to more investors. I believe PFE is close to a real, fully-fledged recovery in the medium term, which is what all bulls have been patiently waiting for. I have no choice but to reiterate my "Buy" on PFE again.

Reviewing Pfizer's Q2 FY2025

For Q2, the firm reported revenues of ~$14.7 billion, a 10.3% YoY operational increase that beat analyst consensus estimate by ~$1.12 billion (~8.3%), and it was one of the biggest beats over the past 2-3 years, based on Seeking Alpha data. And it doesn't seem to be something temporary in nature, as the strength in the top line came from a broad-based growth in both U.S. and international end markets, which actually absorbed the $825 million unfavorable impact from the IRA Medicare Part D redesign (in other words, the sales could have been even bigger if not for that adjustment).

But what I liked more was PFE's continued success in delivering on its cost realignment program: With adj. OPEX being down by 8% YoY, the non-GAAP diluted EPS came in at $0.78, beating the consensus estimate by almost 36%, aligning well with the beats we've been seeing over the past 2 years:

The management noted on the Q2 earnings call that Pfizer remains on track to deliver at least $4.5 billion in net cost savings by the end of this year and a cumulative $7.7 billion by 2027, and that should theoretically free up a lot of capital for further R&D allocation.

As part of our goal to return to pre-pandemic operating margin, we remain on track to deliver on our goal of at least $4.5 billion in cumulative net cost savings from our ongoing cost realignment program by the end of this year. As a reminder, in total, we expect approximately $7.7 billion in savings by the end of '27 to drive operating efficiencies, strengthening our business with the potential of contributing significantly to our bottom line over the period. Of these savings, approximately $500 million identified in R&D will be reinvested in the pipeline, which we expect by the end of '26.

Source: From PFE's Q2 Earnings call

PFE's established blockbusters and newly launched products continue to shape its future growth recovery story, independent of the regulatory headwinds and LOEs. I'm stating it because the firm's revenue increased by 15% from 1H 2024 to 1H 2025, mainly thanks to the recent launches and acquired products, so the upcoming LOEs should be largely offset over time as they come to reality.

I like the confidence that was seen in the management's words and their updated guidance: For FY2025, they raised the EPS range to $2.90-3.10 (the updated midpoint exceeds the previous one by 3.44%), while keeping the top line target unchanged for now.

That new range should be immune to the upfront payment for the 3SBio transaction, which will impact the EPS by $0.20, based on the commentary.

Now given our strong performance to date as well as our outlook, including a favorable impact on foreign exchange, our more efficient cost structure as well as improvements in our adjusted effective tax rate, we are raising our full year '25 adjusted diluted earnings per share guidance by $0.10. This includes absorbing a $0.20 charge for acquired in-process R&D associated with the upfront payment for the 3SBio transaction.

So just to clarify, without the 3SBio deal, we would have raised our adjusted diluted earnings per share guidance by $0.30.

Source: From PFE's Q2 Earnings call, emphasis added by Oakoff

And here it becomes interesting, because the Street decided to revise its earnings consensus for Q3 to the downside despite all that:

Diving deeper into Pfizer's quarterly earnings revisions, we see that all analysts were in sync when it came to the Q3 EPS adjustment, while 58% of the same analysts actually decided to raise their top-line expectations (albeit by an insignificant amount).

The timing of the upfront payment for the 3SBio deal is the key to understanding why they decided to lower their EPS estimates so sharply, so I'd not suggest thinking that they were all misreading the commentary and miscalculating the potential Q3 impact on earnings. Anyway, on a larger time frame, switching to annual earnings revisions, we see that the current consensus even sets a premium on the newly updated EPS midrange ($3.06 vs. $3.00), and the revisions were in ~90% cases bullish over the past 90 days:

From all that, it seems to me that the sell-side analysts largely correctly assess PFE's prospective earnings growth, but I'd assume that since Pfizer doesn't want to stop on cost-cutting, the relative-to-consensus EPS outperformance is very likely to continue.

Pfizer's Valuation Update

With the above outperformance assumptions - let's quantify it as a 5-7%, based on the historical annual EPS surprises - I think that PFE is capable of reporting $3.3441 in non-GAAP diluted EPS for FY2026. If so, the implied FY2026 price-to-earnings multiple should drop to ~7.51x by the end of 2026, assuming the current stock price (if my math is correct).

Right now, PFE trades at 13.36x and ~8.2x of TTM and FWD price-to-earnings multiples, respectively, and it seems to be right in the lower range of its historical valuation range:

The valuation repricing doesn't come without a catalyst, which is why the PFE stock price hasn't recovered to its past $30-40/share mark just yet.

We need to see Pfizer's new launches and acquired products absorbing the LOEs and regulatory headwinds, and that's exactly what PFE's Q2 hinted a, in my understanding.

So, this gives me hope that PFE's valuation repricing can come in the next 12-24 months, and I'm talking about a P/E repricing from the 7.51x I calculated for FY2026 to the range of 9-10x (at least). This is where the upside of at least 26.5% lies for PFE buyers at today's stock price.

Risks And Your Takeaway

There are always a lot of reasons why a particular stock, which seems to be very cheap on the surface, doesn't rally immediately. In Pfizer's case, the risks are high, and most of them are related to what the firm is going to go through when LOEs hit the top and bottom lines. I'm not saying that the path is pretty clear - and that's why the market doesn't want to reprice PFE completely just yet, even if it already sees some signs of a successful business refocus. And I may be wrong in my expectations that the repricing comes in the next 1-2 years, or in my core assumption of a sustainable offsetting of LOEs in the future. In reality, everything is much more complicated, and anything can happen to PFE amid the current regulatory environment.

But I remain an optimist. I know that many value investors, who have been buying dips in PFE and other large-cap healthcare are experiencing deep losses today, but it seems to be just a temporary phenomenon that is very likely to come to an end once the first signs of strength "get cemented" by the next few quarterly results.

I believe that the Street is still underestimating the scale, moat, and recovery potential of Pfizer. It doesn't need to show a 100% multiple expansion to shine - anything of 10-15% would be enough, and it would still leave PFE cheaper than it used to be over the past decades.

Seeing at least 26.5% upside from today's price over the next few months, I leave my "Buy" rating unchanged. And the technical analysis suggests that PFE is going exactly there, as the $30-32/share range seems to be the next logical resistance level for the price.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Keysight's Next Challenge: Converting Growth Into Sustained Margin Gains
data.description:
Investment thesis

Keysight Technologies Inc. (NYSE:KEYS) is a global leader in electronic design and test solutions with a strong moat. This led to a decade-long record of revenue growth, strong cash generation, and leadership in EBIT margins and returns on capital. While the balance sheet is strong and demand drivers remain healthy, margins and capital efficiency have shown little long-term improvement. My base-case valuation, assuming no significant margin expansion or better fixed cost control, points to an intrinsic value below the current market price.

Business background

In layman's terms, Keysight manufactures the tools, software, and systems that engineers and scientists use to design, test, and troubleshoot electronic devices.

Over the past decade, while the company has expanded its capabilities through acquisitions, technology upgrades, and a greater software/service component, its core product–market mix has remained stable.

The company is still anchored in two broad domains – communications and the electronics industry. These end markets and their relative contributions have not materially shifted. This is even as the underlying technologies evolved from 4G to 5G/6G, from ICE vehicles to EVs, and from conventional chips to AI-era semiconductors.

The company today reports its performance under 2 business segments.

i) Communications Solutions Group (CSG). This segment builds testing gear and software that keep phone networks, data centers, AI-driven systems, and military & government communications running smoothly.

ii) Electronic Industrial Solutions Group (EISG). This segment provides tools that help car makers, chip companies, and electronics manufacturers develop and verify their products.

Over the past few years, the CSG segment has been the biggest revenue contributor, accounting for about 69% of the 2024 revenue. Keysight is a global company in that the US only accounted for about 1/3 of its revenue over the past decade.

Revenue growth drivers

From 2015 to 2024, the company’s revenue grew at a 6.4% CAGR. As can be seen from the left part of Chart 1, there were 2 years when revenue declined – 2020 and 2024. The former was pandemic-related, while management attributed the 2024 decline to a challenging macroeconomic environment.

Given that the first half of 2025 revenue is about 6% higher than that for the corresponding period in 2024, with a bit higher guided growth rate for Q3 2025, the revenue decline in 2024 seemed like a one-off situation.

Moving forward, I would expect the company to continue with its historical long-term revenue growth trend. This historical topline growth was due to a combination of organic growth and acquisitions.

In the context of organic growth vs acquisitions, the company did not provide consistent annual changes in revenue attributed to these 2 components. But from what was reported, except for 2018, the acquisition contribution was small.

This seems to be an anomaly when I compare the amount spent on acquisitions vs CAPEX. Over the past decade, the company incurred $ 3.7 billion on net acquisitions compared to $ 1.3 billion in net CAPEX.

I would have thought that a bigger portion of the revenue growth would be attributed to acquisitions. This disconnect suggests that the benefit of the acquisition is better captured in mix, moat, and resilience, and not in the headline growth rate.

Taking this to the logical conclusion, it meant that over the past decade, a big part of the topline growth was driven by organic growth. This is not surprising, as in its March 2023 Investor Day Presentation, the company reported a $ 20.9 billion served addressable market with a 4% to 6% projected growth rate.

Moving forward, the market tailwind would continue as exemplified by the following:

For the CSG segment:

“The global communication test and measurement market size was…$ 9.6 billion in 2024…increasing CAGR of 9.1% during the forecast period (2024 to 2029)” Markets and Markets.

For the EISG segment:

“The global automotive test equipment market size was valued at USD 2.78 billion in 2024 and is expected to expand at a CAGR of 5.2% from 2025 to 2030.” Grand View Research

“Electrical and Electronic Test Equipment Market Size was estimated at 40.78 (USD Billion) in 2023…growth rate expected around 3.44% during the forecast period (2025 - 2035).” Market Research Future

“The Semiconductor Test Equipment Market size was valued at USD 5.85 Billion in 2024…growing at a CAGR of 9.3% from 2026 to 2032.” Verified Market Research

I am confident that the company’s historical growth rate can be sustained. This is because, in addition to a positive market picture, the company has the following competitive edges:

i) Intangible assets. Keysight has proprietary software and patents, positioning it as a leader across core measurement platforms.

ii) Switching costs and customer embeddedness. Once its products and services are embedded into verification/manufacturing, re-qualifying a new vendor risks schedule slips and non-comparable results, raising the cost of switching. Also, close engineering engagement and lifecycle support create recurring touchpoints that competitors struggle to displace.

iii) Cost/scale advantages. Scale manufacturing and centralized fulfillment lower unit costs and improve lead times for complex instruments.

Profit drivers

Unfortunately, the company was not able to translate the 6.4% CAGR in revenue into a higher profit growth rate. Refer to the left part of Chart 1.

For example, from 2015 to 2024, PAT only grew at a 2.0% CAGR with declines in 2017/18 and 2024.

Profits were lower in 2017 mainly due to the amortization of acquisition-related intangibles, fire-related (wildfire) costs, and step-ups in R&D tied to integrating Ixia. In 2018, they stayed below 2016 largely because of a non-cash goodwill impairment.

The 2024’s profit decline was chiefly due to lower revenue and higher Selling, General and Administration (SGA) expenses.

The 2024 profit pressures led to declining returns, with both the ROIC and ROE in 2024 being lower than the respective 2015 values (Chart 1, right)

There are generally 3 drivers of profit growth – scale, margin expansion, and lower fixed costs. Looking at Chart 2, scale seemed to be the main profit driver for Keysight.

The contribution margin, especially post-2020, was relatively flat at 68% for both 2024 and 2020. I would even consider the fixed cost margin as relatively flat. It averaged 46% for 2022 to 2024 compared to the 48% average for 2015 to 2017.

Finally, fixed costs over the past decade were on average about 57% of the total cost. With such a cost structure any changes in revenue would have a big impact on profits. We saw this in 2024, when a 9% drop in revenue led to a 39% drop in operating profit.

Given the strong revenue growth drivers - durable moats, and healthy demand - the base-case path is a return to mid-single-digit revenue growth with faster profit growth from operating leverage.

Notes to Chart 2.

a) Op Profit Profile. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization, R&D, Others
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

b) While the gross profit margin seemed to be improving post-2020, the contribution margin was relatively flat as it was offset by the depreciation & amortization.

c) Similarly, while the SGA margin seemed to be improving (except for 2024), this was offset by depreciation and amortization.

Peer comparison

I compared Keysight’s performance with the following publicly listed peers that were explicitly mentioned as competitors by the company. They have the strongest product-market overlap across RF/microwave, network test, and general electronic test & measurement.

i) Anritsu Corporation (OTCPK:AITUF) (6754). This is a Japan-based maker of RF/microwave instruments and wireless/network test systems. It competes broadly with Keysight in wireless/RF test for 4G/5G/6G and related communications markets.

ii) Fortive Corporation (FTV). This is a diversified industrial technology company; its Tektronix/Keithley/Fluke units sell oscilloscopes, signal sources/analyzers, DMMs, and other electronic test gear. It is a direct head-to-head in core lab/bench instruments and RF test within Keysight’s Electronic, Industrial, and commercial communications markets.

iii) Spirent Communications Plc (OTCPK:SPMYY). This is a UK-listed specialist in communications test/assurance and competes in lab and field validation for wireless, positioning, and high-speed networking - key areas for Keysight’s CSG.

iv) Teledyne Technologies Inc. (TDY) — via Teledyne LeCroy. This is a diversified sensing, instrumentation, and electronics company. Teledyne LeCroy offers high-performance oscilloscopes and protocol analyzers and has a direct overlap in high-end scopes and protocol analysis used across Keysight’s Electronic Industrial Solutions markets.

v) Viavi Solutions Inc. (VIAV). The company provides network test, monitoring, and assurance solutions for telecom and data networks. It overlaps with Keysight in commercial communications and aerospace/defense network test and validation.

As shown in Table 1, Keysight is among the bigger players based on the 2024 revenue. It also had one of the better revenue growth rates. Note that 2 of the peers’ revenues declined during the review period.

Note to Table 1: From Oct to Oct

Overall, Keysight’s peer comparison shows a company that, despite some mid-period setbacks, has steadily strengthened its competitive standing in profitability and cash generation.

While its return on capital and EBIT margin rankings dipped sharply in the late 2010s, both metrics rebounded strongly. The post-2019 EBIT margins surpassed the 2015 level, and returns regained the top spot by 2023–2024. Free cash flow margins and EPS have generally been better than average.

Refer to Charts 3 and 4 for the details:

i) Keysight started initially with the best return on capital. But this deteriorated so that it ranked No. 5 in 2017. Thereafter, its position slowly improved so that by 2023 and 2024, it regained its top ranking. But despite this ranking picture, its quantum of return declined so that its 2023 and 2024 levels were lower than those of 2015.

ii) Keysight EBIT margin declined from the No. 2 rank in 2015 to the worst in 2018. However, it improved since then so that from 2021 onwards, it ranked No. 1. From the quantum perspective, its post-2019 EBIT margins were higher than those of 2015.

iii) I would rate Keysight's free cash flow margin as better than average. Except for 2017, when it ranked No. 5, it ranked among the top 3 for the rest of the review period, with a top ranking in 2021 and 2023.

iv) In terms of the quantum of EPS, except for 2017 when it ranked No. 4, Keysight occupied the No. 2 or 3 positions for the rest of the review period. Furthermore, in terms of the EPS growth rate over the past decade, Keysight also ranked No 3. This is a good performance considering that 2 of the peers had a decline in the EPS, while another did not have any growth.

Financial position

I consider Keysight as sound financially based on the following:

i) It had $ 3.1 billion in cash as of the end of April 2025, equivalent to about 30% of its total assets.

ii) It had a 51% debt equity ratio at the end of April 2025. This has come down from its past decade high (2017) of 89 %.

iii) From 2015 to 2024, it generated positive cash flow from operations every year. It also had a good cash flow from operations conversion ratio. Over this period, it generated $ 8.6 billion in cash flow from operations compared to its cumulative PAT of $ 6.1 billion.

iv) From 2015 to 2024, it had a Reinvestment rate (Reinvestment/NOPAT) of 56%. I defined Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in net Working Capital.

This is a good rate considering that it included acquisitions. Excluding the acquisitions and divestitures, the Reinvestment was negative as the Depreciation & Amortization more than offset the CAPEX.

v) It has a good capital allocation plan as per Table 2. The cash flow from operations was more than enough to cover its acquisitions and CAPEX. Excess was returned to shareholders in the form of buybacks.

Valuation

My analysis showed the 2024 revenue decline is a one-off situation and that the company would continue on its long-term topline growth path. The challenge for the company is that while financially sound, there has not been long-term improvement in contribution margin or better fixed cost control.

As such, I adopted the following picture in valuing the company.

The base revenue would be the 2024 revenue with the Year 1 revenue growth rate of 6%. This is the upper end of the Q2 2025 guidance for the Q3 revenue growth rate. The revenue growth rate would reduce proportionately to the long-term GDP growth rate of 4% in the terminal year (Year 6).

The based contribution margin and fixed cost margin would be the respective 2021 to 2024 average values. These margins would remain the same over the valuation period.

The base capital turnover would be the 2021 to 2024 average value.

The base Reinvestment would be pegged to the past decade's average Reinvestment margin. This would change proportionately so that in the terminal year, the Reinvestment rate would follow that given by the fundamental growth equation.

The tax rate would be the past decade's average rate, excluding 2015 (negative rate) and 2018 (> 100% rate).

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of $91 per share compared to the market price of $ 164 per share (14 Aug 2025). There is no margin of safety.

Valuation model

I valued Keysight based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) valuation model as shown in Table 3. The WACC was based on the Google Search for “Keysight Technologies WACC” as summarized in Table 4.

Notes to Table 3.

a. Proportionate change

b. Pegged to growth rate

c. Assumed no change

d. Assumed no change in the fixed cost margin

e. Revenue X Margin and after accounting for Fixed costs

f. Assumed proportionate change

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at the terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

o. Based on the number of diluted shares

Risks and limitations

The intrinsic value of the company depends on the profitability path. While I have assumed topline growth, I have assumed that there was no change in the margins. My assumptions are not aggressive. In the terminal year, my valuation model would result in the following.

i) 19% return on capital. Looking at the peer returns as per the left part of Chart 3, this would mean that the company maintains its top ranking in 2024, but with a higher level of return

ii) 22% EBIT margin. Based on the 2024 peer performance (Chart 3, right), Keysight would still rank No. 1 but with a higher level of EBIT margin.

Any valuation upside would come from improving margins. For example, if the base contribution margin and base fixed cost margin were 15% better in the terminal year, with proportionate improvements in between, we would have the market price.

I would advise you to look at Chart 2 and see whether such improvements seem possible. Furthermore, the gross profit margin for the first half of 2025 was lower than that for the respective half in 2024.

Finally in my valuation, I had assumed some improvement in the capital efficiency. I had assumed that the Reinvestment rate would reduce from the historical average of 56 % down to 12% in the terminal year. Again, the track record of improving capital efficiency has not been good. You can see from the left part of Chart 1 that there has been no improvement in gross profitability (gross profit/total assets) over the past decade.

With Q3 FY2025 results imminent, the main near-term risk is that reported numbers could diverge from the thesis. A slowdown in growth or deterioration in margins would challenge the assumption that Keysight can sustain profitability gains. Conversely, evidence of margin expansion, improved capital efficiency, or stronger-than-expected demand could point to upside not fully captured in the base-case valuation.

Conclusion

Keysight operates in structurally attractive markets. These would continue to support mid-single-digit revenue growth for the company. Its decade-long record shows resilient demand, stable contribution margins, and a solid balance sheet, but also limited structural margin expansion and uneven capital efficiency.

Peer comparisons confirm Keysight’s strong competitive position. Its recent EBIT margins, free cash flow generation, and returns on capital are among the best in the sector. Yet, the flat contribution margin trend and high fixed cost base suggest that further profitability gains will rely more on disciplined execution than on market growth alone.

My base-case valuation offers no margin of safety. Upside exists if management can deliver sustained margin improvement, but history does not yet demonstrate this trajectory. As such, I would not consider this an investment opportunity from a long-term value investment perspective.

data.title: Waterdrop: Revenue Surprise, But Smaller Upside Stock Price Potential (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In my previous article about Waterdrop Inc. (NYSE:WDH), I noted that the company's AI-driven customer experience could accelerate future business results. The company delivered a beneficial quarter with better than expected revenue growth and lower marketing expenditures. With growth in the number of new patients thanks to the company's E-Find Platform, new products such as the Million Medical Insurance product could push revenue growth in the coming years. I also appreciate that the company delivered a new upgrade of Jiehaoyun and recent information about stock purchases at close to $1.9 per share. Putting everything together, the results of my DCF do not indicate such large upside potential right now. The stock price increased close to 65% from around $1.09 in 2024.

Waterdrop Delivered A Beneficial Quarter In Terms Of Technical Service Income And Medical Crowdfunding Services

Waterdrop Inc. offers insurance and healthcare services, medical crowdfunding services, and enrollment in clinical trials among other options. I covered the company in a recent article, so investors can receive a lot more information about the company's business model from my previous report.

I suppose that investors out there may be quite happy about the most recent results. The company reported better than expected quarterly revenue. Quarterly revenue stood at $105 million. EPS expectations seem quite impressive. In 2026, EPS growth is expected to be close to 47%, the company trades at 10x 2026 EPS. What is more, 2025 revenue is expected to be 20%, and 2026 revenue could be around 8%.

The new financial statements reveal that most of the company's business segments reported revenue growth. First, the technical service income grew by 8.4% year-over-year as compared to that in the same quarter in 2024. The increase in revenue was driven by increases in insurance brokerage income. The company's medical crowdfunding services also saw an increase in revenue of 3.1%. Finally, the company's enrollment in clinical trials and the company's digital clinical trial solution experienced a revenue increase of 11.5% as compared to that in the same quarter in 2024. Considering these results, in my personal opinion, the recent increase in the stock price makes sense.

The Upgrade of Jiehaoyun And The Million Medical Insurance Product Could Accelerate Business Growth

Waterdrop appears to benefit substantially from the use of technologies to communicate with patients and recruit new patients. In my estimation, the existence of the company's E-Find Platform is one of the best reasons to understand why the total enrollment cumulatively stood at 11,217 in March 2025. Further investments in technology could accelerate enrollment and enhance future revenue growth.

There is more. The recent upgrade of Jiehaoyun to version 5.0 and the introduction of the Million Medical Insurance product could also contribute to future revenue growth. Thanks to the new product of the company, the patients seem to sign a health declaration waiver and guaranteed renewal for five consecutive years. In the last press release, the company noted that the new product represented a revolution.

With A Decrease In Marketing Expenditures, Net Profit Increased

With quarterly revenue growth, the company also noted a significant decrease in outsourced sales and marketing service fees and share-based compensation expenses. As a result, sales and marketing expenses decreased by 5.3%. That's not all. General and administrative expenses also decreased by 15.8% year over year. Putting everything together, with revenue growth and a substantial decline in marketing expenditures, future free cash flow and net income could trend higher. The fact that the company can lower marketing expenditures and also report revenue growth is quite beneficial. I assume that new clients would appreciate the company's innovations, technology, and medical crowdfunding services. With the current innovations, Waterdrop appears to be in a position to grow organically in the coming years.

My DCF Model Considering My Own Assumptions, And Previous Financial Statements

Waterdrop reported positive free cash flow of $84 million in 2022 and $23.9 million in 2023. Given the recent decrease in marketing expenditures, revenue growth, the new product Million Medical Insurance product, and the upgrade of Jiehaoyun, I would expect further free cash flow from 2025 to 2035.

My DCF model used 2025 FCF of $22 million, $43 million in 2029, and $58 million in 2035. Other inputs include a discount of 6%, cost of debt of 5%, beta of 0.7x, and net debt of -$261 million.

  • Cost Of Debt: 5%
  • WACC: 6%
  • Beta: 0.7
  • NPV of FCF: $333.01 million
  • NPV of TV: $229.22 million
  • Total EV: $562.23 million
  • Net Debt: -$261 million
  • Equity: $823.23 million
  • Shares: 362 million
  • Target Price: $2.27

According to the most recent press release, the company acquired a total of 54.2 million ADSs for a total sum of $106 million in 2021, 2022, 2023, and 2024. It means that the company acquired shares at approximately $1.9 per share. The current stock price is not far from the price at which Waterdrop bought most of its shares. Considering the results of my DCF and the price at which Waterdrop bought shares, I do not see such a large upside potential right now. Hence, I decided to close my position in the stock.

Pursuant to the share repurchase programs launched in September 2021, September 2022, September 2023 and September 2024, respectively, we had cumulatively repurchased approximately 54.2 million ADSs from the open market with cash for a total consideration of approximately US$106.6 million as of May 31, 2025. Source: 6-k

Risks From A Potential Increase In The Value Of The U.S. Dollar, And Other Risks

From where I stand, I cannot make forecasts about future changes in the forex markets. With that, it is worth mentioning that a potential decline in the value of the USD as compared to the currency in China could harm investors of Waterdrop. It is worth considering that the business model includes assets in China. Hence, even if the company reports net income growth, a decline in the value of USD could lead to losses for investors in the United States.

There are many other risks coming from the fact that Waterdrop operates in China. Judges in the United States may not be able to enforce actions against directors of Waterdrop because they are located in China. With most assets in China, judges may also not be able to enforce actions with regard to the company's digital assets. Investors may perceive a lot of risks because of these matters. Hence, the demand for the stock may not be as large as that received by other companies located in the U.S.

Waterdrop could also suffer from new legislation in the United States against companies from China trading in the United States. There are many regulations that may harm the company's business model. First, the SEC or any other regulator could require a lot of information from the company in order to sell shares in the market. Changes with regard to the necessary audit process in China could also have an adverse impact. In my view, considering other cases in the market about companies in China, most investors would perceive a lot of risks.

Risks From The Corporate Governance Practices In Cayman Islands And The Fact That Waterdrop Is A Controlled Entity

Waterdrop was registered in the Cayman Islands, where corporate governance practices and regulations are different from those in the United States. I reckon that directors in the Cayman Islands have different obligations as compared to that in the U.S. Besides, the securities legislation is not that developed. Finally, I doubt that shareholders could launch class actions against the company. In sum, shareholders are not as protected as in the United States, which may lower the demand for the stock.

Waterdrop is also a controlled company because the Chairman of the Board of Directors and Chief Executive Officer own more than 50% of the total voting power. With this in mind, I would not expect the Board of Directors to be independent, which brings a number of problems. If Waterdrop receives an acquisition offer, the CEO could block the deal, which could not be ideal for minority investors. In addition, if Waterdrop underperforms, changing directors or management may not be that easy. In sum, I suppose that many shareholders may not buy shares because of the fact that Waterdrop is a controlled entity.

Risks From The Relationship With Tencent

According to the last 20-F form, Tencent Group is one of the company's shareholders. Besides, Tencent received payment processing fees from Waterdrop. Waterdrop also provides advertising services to Tencent Group, and there are also cloud technology services. I see a significant number of conflicts of interest in these relationships. We cannot really know whether Waterdrop chose Tencent because it is the best supplier of services or only because Tencent is a shareholder. As soon as the company becomes a bit more independent, new investors will most likely have a look at the company.

Risks From Changing Regulations In Mainland China

Waterdrop operates in China, where the company's subsidiaries need to obtain licenses and permits from the authorities. Among other licenses, there are permits to conduct insurance brokerage business as well as licenses to offer internet information services. In the future, new regulations or new interpretation of the law could bring a number of issues. If Waterdrop loses some of its licenses, or needs to apply for more permits, I expect large declines in revenue expectations or FCF expectations.

My Opinion

Waterdrop delivered outstanding quarterly results including revenue growth and lower marketing expenditures. Besides, I expect revenue growth driven by the upgrade of Jiehaoyun and the new Million Medical Insurance product. For me, Waterdrop is an innovative player. I will be monitoring future developments. With that being said, I am not a buyer of shares at the current price mark. Considering the results of my DCF model and the stock repurchases at approximately $1.9 per share, I no longer see an upside stock price potential. Finally, given the risks concerning the incorporation in the Cayman Islands and potential new regulations in China, I decided to close my position in the stock. I will be studying the stock price to buy back again in the future.

data.title: Mega-Cap Crowding Steers Managers To Japan, Europe
data.description:
5 Key Macro Trends

1. Trade optimism is boosting risk appetite.

Progress in trade discussions between the U.S. and key trading partners has helped spark a rally in mega-cap and speculative small-cap stocks. Simultaneously, rising market confidence has led hedge funds to buy back shares they had bet against.

2. Value investors are leaning into AI.

They are increasing exposure to companies further down the value chain, such as semiconductor equipment, industrial automation, software and professional services businesses. This reflects a long-term outlook on the AI cycle, with investments focused on building and improving AI infrastructure - including data centers and power utilities.

3. Geopolitical shifts are impacting markets.

In Europe, increased spending driven by geopolitical changes is boosting the military-industrial complex. In the U.S., uncertainty around tariffs and economic policies is affecting market stability and weakening the U.S. dollar. This is fueling greater interest in emerging markets.

4. Uncertainty is reflected in the performance of market sectors.

The U.S. healthcare sector, for instance, faces pressure from policy changes, while the energy sector remains optimistic about natural gas due to long-term growth prospects. In finance, large, diversified banks are favored.

5. Real estate and infrastructure may be in recovery.

With interest rates potentially at peak levels, both of these sectors are showing signs of a rebound. Listed REITs are trading at a discount to non-listed real estate and private infrastructure, which investors are watching closely. Government policy trends are boosting renewable energy and midstream waste and energy sectors.

5 Key Equity Manager Insights

Global

Crowded trades remain a challenge.

Managers see the largest mega-cap growth stocks as crowded trades, with many holding less exposure due to high valuations and limited transparency. Meanwhile, they view markets outside the U.S., like Europe and Japan, as attractive for their diversification benefits and potential advantages amid U.S. tariff and currency challenges.

United States

Managers are cautious on the Magnificent Seven.

Although most recognize the strong competitive advantages of this group, they are hesitant to pay premium prices for these stocks due to rising capital demands and uncertainty around long-term profitability and returns.

Emerging Markets

Chinese stocks still look attractive.

Although investor appetite has been mixed on the back of tariff threats and long-term structural issues, value managers agree that Chinese valuations continue to appear attractive. In addition, the Chinese government is expected to add further stimulus if growth falls short of target.

Europe and UK

Managers see more opportunities for outperformance.

They expect the recent outpacing of European stocks relative to U.S. stocks - narrowly driven by banks and defense companies - to widen in the months ahead. This is due to a favorable macroeconomic backdrop of cooling inflation and lower interest rates.

Japan

Domestic-focused stocks are preferred.

Many Japan managers favor companies that benefit from domestic consumer demand rather than exporters. This is due to concerns over trade tariffs and currency volatility that could negatively impact international sales.

Exploring New Frontiers

The current macro trends and manager insights highlight a market landscape shaped by trade optimism, AI-driven investment shifts, and geopolitical uncertainties. Ultimately, managers are cautiously navigating crowded sectors while seeking opportunities in regions beyond the United States, guided in part by valuations.
