data.title: High-Yield Closed-End Funds (Part 4): HIO
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

We continue our series with high-yield closed-end funds, most of which trade at a discount. Today, we talk about Western Asset High Income Opportunity Fund Inc. (HIO).

Fund Overview

HIO was launched on 11/16/1993. It has total assets of around $386.4 million, allocated primarily to the "Consumer Cyclical" industry (21.84%), "Communications" (17.44%), "Energy" (13.72%), and "Finance" (9.69%). HIO has 313 holdings. The fund has a market capitalization of approximately $377 million. The average coupon is 7.34%. The 5-year total return on NAV is 3.89%, and the 10-year total return is 3.98%.

The biggest investment in the portfolio is El Paso Corporation (2.06%):

The credit quality score of the fund is around a BB rating.

The average effective maturity is 6.37 years, with an average effective duration of 3.41 years, so HIO is not overly sensitive.

Distributions

This CEF's distribution rate on NAV is 10.47%. HIO is paying its shareholders a $0.426 yearly dividend in the form of $0.0355 monthly distributions.

Expenses

HIO does not use leverage. The non-leverage expense ratio is 0.9%, including 0.8% management fees and 0.1% other expenses. The fund has a positive 1-year z-score of 0.64.

Historical Premium/Discount

Below in the chart, you can observe a 10-year historical premium/discount of HIO:

HIO is currently trading at a market price of $3.96, with an NAV of $4.07, or a 2.70% discount, which is close to the lowest discount for the past 12 months, 3.80%. Given that the fund is near its lowest valuation in the last year, HIO could represent good value for investors who expect the discount to narrow over time.

And here is the pricing history for the last month:

HIO Vs. HYG

Below is a comparison between the total return of HIO and HYG for the last 10 years:

and for the last 5 years:

There are no two opinions that HIO outperforms HYG. Even if we leverage HYG, the 10-year total return will still be lower than the 10-year total return of HIO:

Conclusion

HIO is a stable and well-structured high-yield fund that is currently trading near its lowest valuation in the last year. With a moderate credit risk and a diversified portfolio, the fund offers a good opportunity. Unlike many other CEF funds in this segment, HIO does not use leverage, making its structure simpler and less susceptible to volatility in interest rate changes. This positions it as a more conservative alternative suitable for investors with a lower risk tolerance. In addition, HIO's historical return outperforms that of HYG, even when the latter is leveraged, which highlights the long-term performance of the fund. If the discount narrows, HIO offers not only current yield, but also the potential for capital gains.

data.title: Microvast: Charging Up Your Portfolio
data.description:
Microvast Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:MVST) came up on one of the stock screens I ran. I was looking for small to mid cap companies that displayed revenue growth of 15% or more in the prior year and expected growth of 15% or more in the upcoming year. I reviewed through some of the companies that this screen populated and thought that Microvast looked like a great stock to review further.

The company caught my attention for a few reasons. One, it operates in a growing market. The company is a battery manufacturer. Particularly I like that it focuses on commercial business and energy storage solutions. It is a more niche market to compete in and I think it will allow the company to turn a profit more quickly. The second, it is past proving it has technology that works. I recently wrote a couple of articles on Arbe Robotics (ARBE) and Enovix Corporation (ENVX), both of which I like, but they are for the most part in the pre-revenue stage. They have a technology, but are still looking to get it to market. I like those stocks for different reasons. Microvast is different in that it already has a product in the market generating revenues. The company has a market cap of under $1 billion but it is not just another speculative tech company hoping that its tech gets picked up by a customer. It is past the point of proving it has technology that works. It is a company with actual revenues and recently positive cash flow. The company has become an expansion and growth story. It is a matter of if it can continue to manage its finances and operations in a way to keep the growth story intact.

Company Overview

Microvast is a vertically integrated manufacturer of advanced battery technology, serving both the commercial electric vehicle (EV) market and the utility-scale energy storage systems (ESS). Founded in 2006 and headquartered in Stafford, Texas, the company has built a global manufacturing footprint that includes facilities in the United States, China, and Germany. Unlike many battery companies that focus on one portion of the value chain, Microvast develops its own battery chemistries: cathode, anode, electrolyte, and separator. It also manufactures cells, modules, and full packs. This level of vertical integration allows tighter control over quality, cost, and supply chain risk, while providing flexibility to tailor solutions for specific customers.

Microvast makes stationary storage systems as part of its ESS business. Its products are also used in a wide range of commercial vehicles including:

  • Light, medium, and heavy-duty trucks

  • Buses and trains

  • Mining trucks, port vehicles, and leisure boats

  • trucks, specialty vehicles, and stationary storage systems.

Its technology portfolio emphasizes fast charging, long cycle life, and improved safety. These attributes are increasingly demanded by fleet operators and utilities. While the company has historically been associated with commercial vehicle OEMs in China and Europe, it has been working to expand its North American footprint, supported by new capacity investments and a broadened customer base.

Financial Review

The company delivered strong top-line performance in the first half of the year. Proving there is a growing market for its products as well as its production capabilities. In Q1 2025, Microvast posted record revenue of $116.5 million, representing a 43.2% increase compared to the same period last year. Q2 results came in at $91.3 million, a more modest 9.2% year-over-year gain. The slowdown in growth concerned investors, and for good reason, but the company did reiterate its full-year forecast for the year. For the first 6 months the company had topline revenue growth of 25.9%. The other positive for the quarter was an improvement in gross margins, up to 34.7%, an improvement of 2.2 points. For the year the company has seen its gross profit grow by 68%. Thanks to the growing revenues and growing margin. This margin expansion suggests early success in optimizing production costs and scaling operations.

The real positive coming out of the quarter is the profitability and cash flow. The company had a positive operating profit of $35 million over the first 6 months of 2025 compared to a loss of $121 million in 2024. The company has seen its operating expenses drop drastically over the past year. The biggest factor in that is due to stock based compensation decreasing as well as an impairment cost in the prior year. The company has also been able to cut costs and did so with a bigger revenue figure on the topline.

The other part of this is EBITDA and cash flow. The company generated $25.9 million in EBITDA for the quarter, a substantial improvement from the negative $78.4 million recorded in the prior year. The company was able to generate $44.3 million in cash from operations in the first 6 months of 2025. It increased its cash position to $138.8 million to end the quarter.

One concern I have on the financials is that the backlog has decreased since the beginning of the year. At the start of the year the company had a backlog of $401 million, that backlog had decreased to $320 million at the end of Q2. The company is converting revenue faster than it is booking new contracts. That is not a recipe for long term growth. There is obviously still around 9 months' worth of revenue backlog on the books (without any additional revenue sources), but this is something worth watching. Especially since the company is bringing additional manufacturing capacity online.

Outlook

Management’s guidance for full-year 2025 revenue of $450 million to $475 million. This guidance is consistent with what management provided at the beginning of the year. It would show strong growth for the year. With revenues of $207 million through the first 6 months the company is on track to meet this goal for the year.

The company is also expanding its manufacturing capacity with Phase 3.2. This will add 2 GWh of manufacturing capacity annually. It is expected to come online in Q4 of this year. The company currently has 3.2 GWh of capacity so this expansion will increase the capacity by 57%. This will allow the company to continue its growth. If you take the revenue earned in 2024 divided by manufacturing capacity or GWh then you get $108.5 million per GWh of capacity. If we extrapolate that into 2026 with the new capacity of 5.5 GWh then you get revenue of $596.8 million for the year. This is very close to estimates that analysts have of $593.9 million. This is good for revenue growth of nearly 29% for the year. If you take the same capacity of 3.5 and use the low end estimate of $450 million for 2025 (the company will not bring the new capacity on until end of year so I do not think it will have much effect on the 2025 results) your get $128.6 million per GWh, the company has been able to increase its utilization. If you extrapolate that with the new capacity of 5.5 then you get revenues of $707.1 million. Obviously increasing capacity by 57% will take some time to fully utilize and book more contracts. It shows the longer-term potential out past next year. I think the outlook of revenue growth is supported by the additional capacity coming online and could even be understating the future growth potential.

The question for me is whether or not the company will be able to win enough contracts to support the new capacity at a good margin. The company saw bookings decrease since the beginning of the year. It will need to win additional contracts in order to fill the new capacity. It has been able to better scale its current operations to improve margins. The challenge will be to bring on the new capacity and still maintain margins.

Cash Position and Going Concern

The company ended the quarter with a cash position of $138. It generated positive cash flow of $44.3 million during the first 6 months. Management has forecast that its operating cash flow will continue to grow as well. The company is moving in the right direction to continue to grow its operations as it is cash flow positive.

The company does have a lot of debt on the books though. It has $117 million in debt with $83 due within the next 12 months. It also has $260 million in other current liabilities. This is brought up in detail in the “Going concern” section in the company’s filings. I agree with the company’s conclusion in that section: “the Group concluded that it is probable that management’s plans will alleviate the substantial doubt about the Group’s ability to continue as a going concern and that adequate sources of liquidity will exist to fund the Company’s working capital and capital expenditures requirements and to meet its short-term debt obligations, and other liabilities and commitments as they become due.”

Management's plans to alleviate the doubt are two fold: Forecasted cash inflow from operations and refinancing short-term debt. We have discussed how the company has become cash flow positive. This is obviously the biggest gain as it can use that cash flow for investments rather than taking on more debt or even use it to pay down debt if there is enough cash. I don’t think there will be any issue refinancing the debt either. In fact the company should be able to refinance much easier and at potentially lower rates as it continues to improve its profitability.

I do not see going concern as a risk. The company has reached a positive cash flow state. They also have a lot of room to grow without significant capital investment as the Phase 3.2 is in the final stages of completion. This will allow a large increase in capacity with most of the capital investment already outlaid. Capital investments do not need to be large and the company can still grow.

If the company wants to continue to expand its operations it will either have to do it slowly with cash flows (not good in a rapidly advancing space), or it will have to find financing or do an equity raise. Microvast has already run into this issue with its manufacturing facility in Clarksville, TN. The company purchased the building and began renovations to start manufacturing there, but it ran out of cash to complete it. The company has noted that it will stay idle until it is able to raise financing to complete the build out. My first concern is that the company is not able to raise financing to complete this project. A company the size of Microvast should be able to obtain financing to complete this project. We are talking about financing backed with physical assets. I think there are a few things going on here, first the company may not want to move too fast on that expansion due to demand at the moment. Second, the company up to this year has not been cash flow positive, making it much harder to get loans, and also the concern of repayment. The terms might not be very good either. The last thing is the company notes that their cash is held overseas. If they want to use that cash in the US they will have to repatriate the money and pay taxes on it. They do not want to do this. They would much rather get financing, which is tax advantageous rather than negative. I think this concern will be resolved as the company continues to grow and be cash flow positive. It will be much easier for the company to obtain financing if it has a stronger balance sheet and stronger cash flows. Also as an investor I hate to say it but depending on the demand and growth trajectory of the business it might be worth an equity raise. If demand is exploding and the company needs the additional capacity then depending on the terms it would be better to do an equity raise and get the return through the new capacity.

Price and Valuation

The company has seen its stock price explode 800+% in the past year. The company went public via SPAC in 2021. The stock price fell into the penny stock realm. The stock traded for under $0.20 a share in November of 2024. It makes the 800% seem less intimidating as it was off such a small base. This climb in the stock price seems to correlate directly with the company's growing revenues and showing signs of profitability.

The stock currently trades at $2.84 at time of writing. That is good for a PE ratio of 15.21 based upon 2025 estimated EPS of $0.19. Next year the company is estimated to have earnings of $0.29, good for a forward PE of less than 10. That is a very cheap valuation for a company growing earnings at 30% and revenue at over 20%. The low valuation comes from the risks around growth, execution, and also capital position. The company carries a lot of debt. So while it has a low PE ratio the EV/Sales ratio is above the sector median. I think this concern will begin to improve now that the company is cash flow positive. It is not as much of a concern for me. The company saw its stock price take a hit after earnings as the declining backlog and revenue miss were concerning. I think these risks are taken into account with the current price though.

Quant rating had the company as a buy before earnings. Following earnings the rating dropped to a hold.

Risks

The key risks for Microvast remain centered on execution and financial structure. While the company is generating positive cash flow through the year, is it enough to allow the company to pay its debts and still expand. Any delays in scaling production, cost overruns, or unexpected shifts in demand could slow or derail its progress.

Additionally, as a global manufacturer, Microvast is exposed to geopolitical and trade risks, particularly given its operations in China and the current volatility in US/China relations. Currently all the manufacturing is done in China. There is the concern of tariffs hurting margins going forward.

Conclusion

Microvast’s investment case hinges on whether it can successfully execute its scale-up strategy. It needs to make sure it can maintain its margins and keep enough business flowing through the door as it expands capacity. The company is in a growth stage but it also has to be prudent with its growth to assure it does not get into a cash crunch. Growing cash flows and profitability are moving things in the right direction. I think the company will be able to better utilize debt markets as the financials improve.

Secular tailwinds in both the commercial EV and utility-scale storage markets support the long-term growth story. Electrification of fleets is accelerating globally, and grid operators are increasingly investing in storage solutions to stabilize renewable energy generation.

For growth-oriented investors willing to tolerate volatility and execution risk, Microvast presents an opportunity. If management can deliver on its revenue and margin guidance, the upside could be substantial. Conversely, for more risk-averse investors, the approach may be to remain on the sidelines until profitability and balance sheet strength are more firmly established. I have taken this approach and it has cost me money over the years and also saved me on some losses. The upside outweighs the risks for me. I am a buyer of MVST.
